questions.a: Data where each example is paired with an output label, used to train supervised models
Subset of labeled data used to train a model
Subset of labeled data used to evaluate a model’s performance
Subset of data used to fine-tune model hyperparameters during training
Model’s ability to perform well on unseen data
Model is too simple to capture the underlying patterns in the data
Model is too complex and captures noise in the training data
Technique for assessing model performance by training and testing on different data splits
The surface that separates different predicted classes in the input space
Predicts a continuous output using a linear relationship between input features
Predicts probabilities for binary classification using the logistic function
Tree-structured models that split data based on feature values to make predictions
Classifier that finds the optimal hyperplane separating different classes
Classifies instances based on the most common label among the nearest neighbors
Proportion of correct predictions out of total predictions made
Proportion of true positives out of all predicted positives
Proportion of true positives out of all actual positives
Harmonic mean of precision and recall; balances both in one metric
Matrix that shows the counts of true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives
Matrix capturing the covariances between each pair of features
Functions used to measure the error between predicted and actual values
Mean Squared Error - Average of squared differences between predictions and actual values
Loss function that penalizes incorrect, confident predictions. Effective for classification tasks
L = -[y * log(p) + (1 - y) * log(1 - p)] — used for binary classification
L = -sum over classes of (y_i * log(p_i)) — used for multiclass classification
